title: Ruth Bader Ginsburg Passes Away at 87
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds to the September 2020 death of Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg by arguing that Roe is unlikely to be overturned even by a Republican-stacked court, and that abolitionists' duty to defy the court's pro-abortion order remains unchanged regardless of who fills the vacancy.
audiences: abolitionists weighing the importance of Supreme Court vacancies
pro-life conservatives placing hope in Republican judicial appointments
Christians wrestling with obedience to God versus deference to the courts
state legislators considering nullification of Roe v. Wade
political observers tracking the post-Ginsburg confirmation fight

questions.question: Why does Silberman think appointing an anti-Roe justice would not necessarily lead to Roe's reversal?
questions.audience: pro-life conservatives placing hope in Republican judicial appointments
questions.answer: He points to the 1992 Planned Parenthood v. Casey decision, where a court of eight Republican appointees and a pro-life Democrat still upheld Roe. History shows Republican-stacked courts repeatedly decline to overturn abortion precedent, so a sixth Republican appointee does not guarantee a different outcome.
questions.quote: In 1992, the US supreme court was made up of eight Republican nominees, including three from Reagan and two from Bush. The lone Democrat nominee on the court was Justice Byron White who dissented in *Roe v. Wade*. So there were eight Republicans and a pro-life Democrat. The *Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992)* decision should have been a slam-dunk overturning of *Roe*. Yet even the '92 court upheld legal abortion.

questions.question: Which sitting justice has Silberman identified as the only one to publicly call for Roe to be overturned?
questions.audience: political observers tracking the post-Ginsburg confirmation fight
questions.answer: Clarence Thomas. Silberman notes that Thomas has consistently written separate opinions urging the court to scrap its abortion jurisprudence, including in June Medical Services v. Russo, but no other sitting justice has joined him.
questions.quote: The only current justice who has ever publicly stated that *Roe* should be overturned is Clarence Thomas. Thomas has written separate opinions calling for the court's abortion jurisprudence to be overturned in nearly every abortion-related case that has come before him, including the recent *June Medical Services v. Russo* decision. These opinions have not been joined by any other current justice.

questions.question: Why does Silberman insist that Ginsburg's death changes nothing for abolitionists' duties?
questions.audience: abolitionists weighing the importance of Supreme Court vacancies
questions.answer: Because the moral obligation to refuse cooperation with mass murder is grounded in God's word and equal protection, not in the composition of the court. The court's order remains the same and the moral standard remains the same, so the states' obligation to disregard Roe stands regardless of who occupies the bench.
questions.quote: Even if an anti-*Roe* judge gets appointed, nothing changes for abolitionists. As Christians who strive for obedience to God's word and to a lesser extent, as Americans who care about equal protection under the Constitution, when we're ordered to allow mass murder, we're obligated to say 'no.' Ginsburg has passed away, but the court's order to the states that they must allow mass murder in their jurisdictions hasn't changed, and the objective standard of morality hasn't changed.

questions.question: What does Silberman, citing Russell Hunter, identify as the real ideology that needs to die in the fight against abortion?
questions.audience: Christians wrestling with obedience to God versus deference to the courts
questions.answer: Not any particular justice, but the ideology of unconditional submission to the Supreme Court regardless of how unjust or irrational its rulings are. The battle is fundamentally against judicial supremacy itself rather than against any individual on the bench.
questions.quote: As my colleague Russell Hunter [said](https://www.facebook.com/FreeTheStates/posts/1035683880197396) on Facebook, the key to the battle against abortion isn't about anyone dying or leaving the court. It's about an ideology of unconditional subservience to the court against all reason and humanity that needs to die.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the upcoming nomination fight following Ginsburg's death?
questions.audience: political observers tracking the post-Ginsburg confirmation fight
questions.answer: He expects it to be even more bitter than the Brett Kavanaugh confirmation, citing Mitch McConnell's immediate announcement that Trump's nominee would receive a Senate floor vote.
questions.quote: US Senate majority leader Mitch McConnell has already released a statement revealing that "President Trump's nominee will receive a vote on the floor of the United States Senate." The upcoming nomination battle is likely to make the Kavanaugh confirmation battle look tame by comparison.

questions.question: What course of action does Silberman urge for state governments regardless of the court's makeup?
questions.audience: state legislators considering nullification of Roe v. Wade
questions.answer: States must move toward total and immediate abolition of abortion in defiance of the Supreme Court rather than waiting for the court to overturn Roe. Waiting on judicial permission to protect preborn humans is itself a dereliction of duty.
questions.quote: The court is still not likely to overturn *Roe*, and even if it were, we would be derelict in our duty as Christians if we waited for them to do so before giving equal protection to preborn humans. Regardless of who fills Ginsburg's former seat on the court, our work and objectives remain the same. We must continue working toward the total and immediate abolition of abortion in defiance of the genocidal United States supreme court.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Directly referenced in this article and provides the deeper argument against judicial supremacy that frames Silberman's response to Ginsburg's death.

related.title: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
related.reason: Covers the June Medical Services v. Russo ruling cited here as evidence that even Republican-appointed justices fail to overturn abortion precedent.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Names the very ideology of court worship that Silberman and Russell Hunter say must die in the fight against abortion.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Articulates the doctrine of state nullification that Silberman explicitly points readers to as the path forward regardless of who replaces Ginsburg.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Defends the historical and constitutional case for state nullification of Roe, the practical alternative to waiting on the Supreme Court.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Engages the pro-life objection that nullification is unrealistic, complementing this article's call for state defiance of the court.
